content.problemStatement:
Even before they leave the apartment, Joey drives Chandler crazy by forgetting everything useful, even his passport, but crying every five seconds "London, baby!", and photographing absolutely everything all the time, as if 6 hours on an airplane with Monica weren't bad enough. On reaching, Joey uses a map to visit the famous places nearby.

Joey is currently at the coordinates (0,0)(0,0)(0,0) and he wants to reach the coordinates (P,Q)(P,Q)(P,Q). Chandler has a set of instructions for Joey to move. He provides Joey a binary string SSS of length NNN. Joey chooses one of the axes : either x-axis or y-axis. Joey then follows the instructions. To follow the ithi^{th}ith instruction:



If SiS_iSi​ = 000, Joey has to change his axis immediately and move in any direction (positive or negative) of the resulting axis by 111 unit.




If SiS_iSi​ = 111, Joey can move in any direction (positive or negative) along the current axis by 111 unit.



For x-axis, moving in positive direction implies moving from (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) to (x+1,y)(x+1, y)(x+1,y) and moving in negative direction implies moving from (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) to (x−1,y)(x-1, y)(x−1,y). Similarly, for y-axis, moving in positive direction implies moving from (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) to (x,y+1)(x, y+1)(x,y+1) and moving in negative direction implies moving from (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) to (x,y−1)(x, y-1)(x,y−1).

Determine if Joey can reach (P,Q)(P,Q)(P,Q) at the end.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of testcases. Then the testcases follow.
First line of each testcase contains of three integers N,P,QN, P, QN,P,Q.
Second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS.


Output Format
For each test case print "YES" if he can reach coordinates (P,Q)(P, Q)(P,Q) at the end, else print "NO".


You may print each character of each string in uppercase or lowercase (for example, the strings "yEs", "yes", "Yes" and "YES" will all be treated as identical).
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains three integers N, P and Q — the length of the binary string and the target coordinates. The second line contains a binary string S of length N consisting only of characters '0' and '1'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Joey can reach (P, Q) after following the instructions, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: Joey starts by choosing the y-axis. S0=1S_0 = 1S0​=1, he chooses to move towards the positive direction of y-axis. S1=1S_1 = 1S1​=1, he chooses to move towards the negative direction of y-axis. S2=1S_2 = 1S2​=1, he moves towards the negative direction of y-axis. Thus, his path is (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) →\rightarrow→ (0,1)(0, 1)(0,1) →\rightarrow→ (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) →\rightarrow→ (0,−1)(0, -1)(0,−1).
Test case 2: Joey starts by choosing x-axis. S0=1S_0 = 1S0​=1, he chooses to move towards the positive direction of x-axis. S1=1S_1 = 1S1​=1, he chooses to moves towards the positive direction of x-axis. S2=0S_2 = 0S2​=0, he changes the axis and chooses to move towards the negative direction of y-axis S3=0S_3 = 0S3​=0, he changes the axis and chooses to move towards the positive direction of y-axis. Thus, his path is (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) →\rightarrow→ (1,0)(1, 0)(1,0) →\rightarrow→ (2,0)(2, 0)(2,0) →\rightarrow→ (2,−1)(2, -1)(2,−1) →\rightarrow→ (2,0)(2, 0)(2,0).

solution.approach: For each test case we only need to know how many moves are performed on the x‑axis and how many on the y‑axis. Let Z_i be the number of '0's in the prefix S[0..i]. The axis used at step i is the initial axis toggled Z_i times. Therefore steps where Z_i is even use the initial axis, steps where Z_i is odd use the other axis. By scanning the string once we count the number of indices with even Z_i (call it cntEven) and odd Z_i (cntOdd = N - cntEven). If the initial axis is x, then x‑moves = cntEven and y‑moves = cntOdd; if the initial axis is y the numbers are swapped. With k moves on a coordinate we can reach any integer x such that |x| <= k and (k - x) is even (because each move contributes +1 or -1). So we check the two possibilities of the initial axis and verify the parity/absolute‑value conditions for P and Q. If either works, answer is YES.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (single scan of the string). Overall O(total N) ≤ 2·10^5.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys

def possible(k, target):
    return abs(target) <= k and ((k - target) & 1) == 0

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        P = int(next(it))
        Q = int(next(it))
        S = next(it)
        cnt_even = 0
        parity = 0  # parity of zero count so far
        for ch in S:
            if ch == '0':
                parity ^= 1
            if parity == 0:
                cnt_even += 1
        cnt_odd = N - cnt_even
        ok = False
        # start with x-axis
        kx, ky = cnt_even, cnt_odd
        if possible(kx, P) and possible(ky, Q):
            ok = True
        # start with y-axis
        kx, ky = cnt_odd, cnt_even
        if possible(kx, P) and possible(ky, Q):
            ok = True
        out_lines.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
